content:
9 min read
Just now

--

The companion essay argued the method. Here you clone a repo and run one reconciliation with an AI agent — scouting the divergence, resolving by intent, and proving a ladder of oracles catches what a clean merge hides.
You have a refactor branch that has been open for weeks, and main has not waited -- it is dozens of commits ahead. Every day you put off the merge the gap grows, and so does the dread: when you finally run git merge you will get a screen of conflict markers in files that have moved, split, and changed shape under you, with no confident way to tell a real collision from noise.
The Merging Volatile Branches essay made the case: keeping a long-lived refactor branch alive against a moving mainline is a reconnaissance problem, not a merge problem. You let git do the merge; the agent scouts the few spots the merge will get wrong; and a ladder of oracles catches whatever slips through. That was the argument. This is the part where you feel it.
There is a small repo that stages the whole thing in miniature — a mainline and a refactor branch that have genuinely diverged — so you can run one reconciliation end to end in about ten minutes. And you do not do it by typing merge commands into a void: you run a loop with an AI agent — it scouts and resolves, you hold the git and the oracles. The exchanges below are condensed from real sessions.
Repo: https://github.com/Netprogs/repo-merge-example
What you are cloning
A toy TypeScript service on three branches:

• main -- the mainline, which kept shipping: a bug fix, a new route, a new type, a couple of tests.

• refactor (default) -- the long-lived refactor: a file moved, a function split into a new module, a data shape flattened, a type retired, a store initializer hardened.

• refactor-merged -- the answer key: everything reconciled, green. Peek only after you try.
The divergence is real git history, small enough to hold in your head but built to exercise all three shapes a merge cannot resolve for itself: moved, split, and reshaped.
Clone it
git clone https://github.com/Netprogs/repo-merge-example reconcile-demo
cd reconcile-demo # you land on refactor, the branch to reconcile
npm install
npm run build # confirm it compiles before you startPrime the agent
Open a fresh AI session and hand it the two playbooks the repo ships:
You:
Consume these for the session: docs/playbooks/session-init-prompt.md and docs/playbooks/branch-reconciliation-prompt.md.
Agent:
Primed and read-only until you give me a task. I will not run git, tsc, tests, the sweep, or the merge myself -- you run those and hand me the output. Reconnaissance does not need me to run anything, only to reason over a trustworthy run. Once you give me a task, I propose a map and, after you merge, edit files to resolve the predicted conflicts -- but I never run the commands.
You have not asked it to do anything yet. You have established who does what: the agent scouts, proposes, and — once you merge — resolves by editing files; you run every command, even the read-only ones, and hand back what they produce. That division is the whole reason this stays safe.
Give it the task; get a map
You:
Run this playbook for the reconcile on the current branch.
The sweep runs from the read-only harness (npm run reconcile), and you run it -- not the agent. A sweep can misfire in an agent's sandbox (a truncated file, a stale view of git), and the essay makes running the tool yourself a standing rule -- reconnaissance only needs the agent to reason over a trustworthy run, not to produce one.
Run npm run reconcile and paste the output back. What comes back is not a wall of diff but a map: the harness sorts the divergence into four buckets -- only-mine, only-old, both-touched, move-conflict -- and adds two derived lines (net-new that arrives via the merge, and raw reshape hits):
Buckets:
only-old (clean bring-forward): 4
both-touched (same path both sides): 3
move-conflicts (renamed away on NEW): 1
only-mine (your refactor surface): 6
outside-scope net-new (comes in via merge): 0
reshape candidates: 3Reading those:

• only-old (4) — the mainline’s work on paths the refactor never touched; the merge brings them in clean. (The essay names this bucket only-sprint, after that team's mainline ref; the shipped harness calls it only-old generically.)

• both-touched (3) — files both sides changed; some auto-merge, some collide.

• move-conflict (1) — a path the refactor renamed away.

• only-mine (6) — the refactor’s own surface. Two of the six are its workstream docs (the plan and a reconciliation tracker under docs/future/engine-refactor/) -- context for the merge, and where this run gets recorded at the end.
(reshape candidates is a raw signature scan; the three hits are tooling that mentions the pattern, not real targets -- you filter those, as you would at scale.)
What will actually conflict
Buckets tell you what diverged; they do not tell you what will collide. A read-only three-way simulation against the merge-base does — you run it and hand back the result, and the agent, reading it, tells you exactly which overlaps will conflict and flags the breaks that no merge and no compiler will show:
Agent:
Not safe to blind-merge. No hard blockers, but a few things must be resolved by intent, not by greening the bar. Textual conflicts: profile.ts (the reshape) and signup.ts (the split); server.ts auto-merges on disjoint hunks.
Then the clean-merge breaks the merge will not show: admin/reset.ts imports a type the refactor retired; store.test.ts builds the store without a clock the refactor now requires; and the refactor's dispatchWelcome ignores isNew, so a repeat signup would send twice and fail main's regression test.
Stop and look at that. Before a single line was merged, you have a falsifiable plan: these two files will conflict, these breaks are lurking, everything else is fine. You can reason about that. You could not reason about a screen of conflict markers.
You merge; the agent resolves — by intent
Merging is yours. On a fresh clone the mainline is the remote-tracking ref:
git merge -X find-renames=30% origin/main
git status > .reconcile-out/merge-status.txtIt leaves exactly the two predicted conflicts. server.ts auto-merges; the auth/login.ts move is auto-followed, carrying main's edit onto modules/auth/login.ts. The map held.
Hand the result back, and the agent works the merged files — reading them, not just clearing markers — and explains each edit by intent:
Agent:
Two textual conflicts. profile.ts -- kept the refactor's flat preferences and folded in main's defensive copy: return profile ? [...profile.preferences] : [];. signup.ts -- took the refactor's dispatchWelcome(...) seam and dropped the stray inline sendWelcomeEmail the merge left behind.
One break merged clean: admin/reset.ts imports SignupRequest, which the refactor retired for Credentials -- swap the import. And two breaks that demand opposite fixes.
store.test.ts -- fix the TEST: it builds the store with no clock, and the refactor now requires one at runtime; that requirement is a deliberate refactor decision, so the test is the stale side -- pass a clock.
signup.test.ts -- fix the CODE: it asserts a repeat signup sends no second welcome (main's bug fix), but the refactor's split left dispatchWelcome sending regardless; the test is right, the code dropped a behavior -- leave the test and gate the send on isNew in welcome-dispatch.ts.
That last pair is the heart of it: same symptom — a test about to fail — and the fix goes opposite ways, one changing the test and the other changing the code. The agent decided each by reading why the mainline wrote the test and why the branch changed the code, never by asking which edit turns the bar green. That is the single discipline this whole exercise exists to build.
Run the oracles
The ladder, bottom to top: the merge -> the simulation -> the compiler -> the tests — each rung catching what the one below can’t (the essay draws it out). Run them — you, not the agent:
npm run build # tsc
npm test # tsc, then node --testWhat happens next depends on how thorough the agent was, and both outcomes are the process working.
Green on the first try — what happened this run. A careful agent working from the map clears the whole thing itself, so the oracles just confirm. Green is the map and the resolution paying off, not luck.
A rung goes red — what happened the first time we ran this. The agent missed admin/reset.ts: a file that merged in perfectly clean, importing the retired SignupRequest. The compiler caught it:
src/admin/reset.ts:1:10 - error TS2305: Module '"../types"' has no exported member 'SignupRequest'.That is not the process failing — it is the net doing its one job. You fix it by intent (swap the import to Credentials), rebuild, and move on.
Either way you run every rung, every time, because you cannot predict which run is the one where something slips.
See the net catch
In a ten-file toy the agent can hold the whole tree in its head, so it usually clears the map and the rungs stay quiet — you may never watch one bite. On a real branch it will miss things, and the ladder is the only reason those do not ship. So make a rung bite on purpose:
Compiler rung — in src/profile.ts, reach back into the old wrapped shape the refactor flattened away:
return profile ? [...profile.preferences.value.data] : [];npm run build goes red: error TS2551: Property 'value' does not exist on type 'string[]'. A change that reads fine and cannot compile -- caught. Restore [...profile.preferences].
Test rung — in src/signup/welcome-dispatch.ts, drop the guard so it sends every time:
export const dispatchWelcome = (email: string, isNew: boolean): void => {
void isNew; // was: if (isNew) { ... }
sendWelcomeEmail(email);
};npm test goes red: a repeat signup ... sends no second welcome -- the break that passes the compiler and only the tests can see. Restore the guard.
That is the ladder’s real job: not to bite on this ten-file example, but to stand exactly there on the day it — or you — miss something in a thousand-file one.
Land the reconcile (green is not done)
Here is where most merge write-ups stop and where the agentic version keeps going. A green bar is a checkpoint, not a record. The reconcile is not finished until the next person — or the next you, three weeks from now — can see what happened and why.
And the place to record it already exists: this refactor branch carries a reconciliation tracker at docs/future/engine-refactor/branch-reconciliation/branch-reconciliation-plan.md, shipped with the workstream. The agent fills the tracker's Map section and appends a delta-log entry for the run -- the one write it makes in an otherwise read-only process:
Agent:
Resolved and green. Recording the run in the reconciliation tracker — merge-base, the buckets, the two files that conflicted, the clean-merge breaks, and how each was resolved:
- 2026-07-16 -- run 1. merge-base <sha>. only-old 4, both-touched 3 (2 conflicted:
profile.ts reshape, signup.ts split), move-conflict 1 (auto-followed), only-mine 6.
Clean-merge breaks: admin/reset.ts (retired SignupRequest -> Credentials); store.test.ts
(clock now required -- fixed the test); welcome-dispatch.ts (dropped isNew guard -- fixed
the code). tsc + tests green; merge committed.You commit the resolution first, then the tracker update as the clean final commit. Now the reconcile is a durable record, not a fading terminal buffer — the living map the essay described, one entry richer. Run this loop on a cadence and that log is the difference between a stack of isolated panics and one process that gets calmer each time.
Point it at your own branch
The two tools are copy-paste portable: docs/scripts/reconcile-branch.js (wired as npm run reconcile) and docs/playbooks/branch-reconciliation-prompt.md. Drop them in your repo and change one line in the harness -- the RESHAPE signature at the top -- to whatever shape your own refactor is flattening.
